perturbation.original_text: 7. Remedies Without prejudice to any other rig hts or remedies that any P arty may have, the Recipient Party acknowledges and agrees that damages alone would not be an adequate remedy for any breach by the Recipient Party of the provisions of this A greeme nt, and that the remedies of injunction and specific performance as well an any other equitable relief for any threatened or actual breach by the Recipient Party or its Representatives would be more appropriate remedies.
perturbation.changed_text: 12. No Partnership  7. Remedies Without prejudice to any other rig hts or remedies that any P arty may have, the Recipient Party acknowledges and agrees that damages alone would not be an adequate remedy for any breach by the Recipient Party of the provisions of this A greeme nt, and that the remedies of injunction and specific performance as well an any other equitable relief for any threatened or actual breach by the Recipient Party or its Representatives would be more appropriate remedies.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the 'Remedies' clause to the 'No Partnership' section obscures its importance and makes it less likely to be noticed, weakening its enforceability.  It's standard practice for remedy clauses to follow term/termination clauses in a clear, easily located section.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Specific performance is discretionary in Irish law and is not always granted. Parties' reasonable expectations regarding remedies is relevant under Irish contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Moving the remedies clause diminishes its prominence and potentially undermines the enforceability of the specific performance remedy. While Irish law recognizes specific performance, its application is discretionary, influenced by the parties' reasonable expectations. By burying the clause, the Recipient Party could argue that it was not adequately highlighted, thereby weakening the claim for specific performance and contradicting reasonable expectations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: specific performance | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you specific performance Specific performance is a contractual remedy in which a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations as closely as possible to what was promised in the contract , rather than simply paying damages for failing to do so. This remedy is often applied when monetary damages are inadequate to fix the harm caused by the breach ; most commonly in cases involving real property and rare chattels . Specific performance ensures that the aggrieved party receives exactly what was agreed upon in the contract, often in situations where the subject matter is unique or irreplaceable. [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS courts business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure commercial transactions courts and procedure property law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a structural flaw that weakens the enforceability of the remedies clause, potentially contradicting reasonable expectations regarding specific performance. The Perturbation Explanation states that moving the clause 'obscures its importance and makes it less likely to be noticed, weakening its enforceability.' The Law Explanation adds that the Recipient Party could argue the clause was not adequately highlighted, weakening the claim for specific performance. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines specific performance as 'a contractual remedy in which a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations as closely as possible to what was promised in the contract.' By burying the remedies clause, the changed text makes it more difficult to ensure that the aggrieved party receives exactly what was agreed upon, creating a legal contradiction under the definition of 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 14. Governing Law This Agreement and any dispute or claim arising out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation (including non -contractual disputes or claims) shall be governed by and construed in accordance with Irish law. The parties irr evocably agree that the courts o f Ireland shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim that arises out of or in connection with this agreement or its subject matter or formation (including non -contractual disputes or claims).
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Term and Termination 14. Governing Law This Agreement and any dispute or claim arising out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation (including non -contractual disputes or claims) shall be governed by and construed in accordance with Irish law. The parties irr evocably agree that the courts o f Ireland shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim that arises out of or in connection with this agreement or its subject matter or formation (including non -contractual disputes or claims).
perturbation.explanation: Placing the governing law clause inside the 'Term and Termination' section creates ambiguity. Governing law clauses are generally at the end for clarity and should be easily found.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Irish law requires contracts to be interpreted to give effect to the parties' intentions, which includes ensuring the governing law is clear and unambiguous.
perturbation.law_explanation: Moving the governing law clause to 'Term and Termination' weakens its enforceability by creating the implication that it only applies during the term of the agreement. Irish law requires contracts to be interpreted based on the parties' intentions, and a clear governing law clause is meant to apply perpetually. By placing it in the term section, it could be argued it's not meant to apply to disputes arising after termination, contrary to the intended scope and Irish contract law principles of interpretation.
perturbation.justification: The structural flaw of placing the governing law clause within the 'Term and Termination' section creates a legal contradiction. As the Perturbation Explanation states, governing law clauses are typically placed at the end for clarity and ease of access. The Law Explanation further elaborates that this placement weakens the enforceability of the clause by suggesting it only applies during the agreement's term. While the provided snippets are empty, the explanations adequately describe how this placement can be interpreted as not applying to disputes after termination, potentially conflicting with the intended scope of Irish contract law, particularly concerning the parties' intentions for the clause's perpetual application. The change introduces ambiguity regarding the clause's lifespan and contradicts the principle that it should govern disputes even after the contract's term.

perturbation.original_text: 5. No rights in and no warranty on Confidential Information 5.1 Each Party acknowledges and agrees that it shall not acquire by implication or otherwise any right or licence on or title to any Confidential Information communicated by or acquired from the other Party; 5.2 Neither Party makes any express or implied representation or warranty as to the accuracy or completeness of any Confidential Information; 4 5.3 Nothing in this Agreement shall require any Party to disclose Confidential Information to the other Party nor limit either Party's right to conduct discussions with third parties so long as such discussions do not breach the terms of this Agreement. 6. Term and Termination
perturbation.changed_text: 1. Definitions and Interpretation 5. No rights in and no warranty on Confidential Information 5.1 Each Party acknowledges and agrees that it shall not acquire by implication or otherwise any right or licence on or title to any Confidential Information communicated by or acquired from the other Party; 5.2 Neither Party makes any express or implied representation or warranty as to the accuracy or completeness of any Confidential Information; 4 5.3 Nothing in this Agreement shall require any Party to disclose Confidential Information to the other Party nor limit either Party's right to conduct discussions with third parties so long as such discussions do not breach the terms of this Agreement. 6. Term and Termination
perturbation.explanation: Moving the 'No rights in and no warranty on Confidential Information' section to the 'Definitions and Interpretation' section weakens the overall clarity of the agreement. This clause dictates the extent of legal warranty provided, which is a central point that shouldn't be embedded in definitions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Under Irish contract law, exclusion and limitation clauses should be prominent and unambiguous to be enforceable.
perturbation.law_citation: Enforceability of Exclusion Clauses in Ireland
perturbation.law_explanation: Irish law requires that limitation of liability clauses (which this section effectively is) be prominently displayed and unambiguous to be enforceable. Moving this clause to the Definitions section obscures it, making it less likely to be noticed and understood. This undermines its enforceability because a party could argue that the limitation was not adequately brought to their attention, contradicting Irish law requirements for exclusion and limitation clauses.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a structural flaw by moving the 'No rights in and no warranty on Confidential Information' section to the 'Definitions and Interpretation' section. As per the perturbation explanation, this placement weakens the clarity of the agreement regarding the extent of legal warranty provided. The Law Explanation states that Irish law requires limitation of liability clauses to be prominently displayed and unambiguous to be enforceable, and moving the clause obscures it. This aligns with the definition of 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction', where a required legal disclosure (in this case, limitation of liability) is placed in an irrelevant or misleading section, making it difficult for parties to locate or interpret. The scraped snippets provide general information about Irish law resources but do not directly address the prominence of liability clauses; however, the Law Explanation fills this gap with a reasonable interpretation of enforceability standards, given the nature of the clause.
